title: Targeting a CAR to the TRAC locus with CRISPR/Cas9 enhances tumour rejection
text_chunk: Eyquem et al. (Nature, 2017) demonstrated a transformative approach to CAR-T cell engineering by using CRISPR/Cas9 to direct a CD19-targeting chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) cassette into the T-cell receptor alpha constant (TRAC) locus. This site-specific integration simultaneously disrupted the endogenous TCR and placed CAR expression under control of the endogenous TRAC promoter, enabling physiological regulation of receptor surface density. The investigators compared TRAC-targeted CAR-T cells against conventionally manufactured CAR-T cells produced by retroviral random integration. In a murine xenograft model of systemic CD19+ acute lymphoblastic leukemia using NALM-6 cells, TRAC-CAR T cells showed markedly superior anti-tumor activity, with significantly delayed tumor progression and improved overall survival compared to retrovirally transduced counterparts. Mechanistically, the TRAC-integrated CARs exhibited dynamic surface regulation analogous to the native TCR: receptor internalization upon antigen encounter followed by re-expression during rest. This prevented tonic signaling, a known driver of premature T-cell exhaustion. Flow cytometry and transcriptomic analyses confirmed that TRAC-CAR T cells maintained a less exhausted phenotype with lower expression of PD-1, LAG-3, and TIM-3 relative to randomly integrated CARs. The study also showed that TRAC-CAR T cells retained greater proliferative capacity and cytokine production upon repeated antigen stimulation. This work established a critical proof-of-concept that precision genome editing can improve CAR-T cell function by exploiting the endogenous transcriptional regulatory machinery, providing a foundation for next-generation CAR-T manufacturing strategies. The approach has influenced subsequent clinical programs exploring site-specific integration at the TRAC locus and other genomic safe harbors.
keywords: CRISPR/Cas9, TRAC locus, site-specific integration, CAR-T, tonic signaling, exhaustion, gene editing, CD19, receptor regulation

title: Long-Term Follow-up of CD19 CAR Therapy in Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia
text_chunk: Park et al. (New England Journal of Medicine, 2018) reported mature follow-up data from a single-center phase 1 trial at Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center evaluating 19-28z CD19-targeted CAR-T cells in 53 adult patients with relapsed or refractory B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia (B-ALL). The CAR construct used a CD28 costimulatory domain. Patients received lymphodepleting chemotherapy with cyclophosphamide followed by infusion of autologous 19-28z CAR-T cells. The complete remission (CR) rate was 83% (44 of 53 patients), with 67% of responders achieving minimal residual disease (MRD)-negative status by multiparameter flow cytometry. Median overall survival was 12.9 months, and the median event-free survival was 6.1 months. Among MRD-negative responders who proceeded to allogeneic stem cell transplantation (allo-SCT), outcomes were significantly better than those who did not, with median overall survival not yet reached. Severe cytokine release syndrome (CRS) occurred in 26% of patients, requiring tocilizumab and/or corticosteroids. Neurotoxicity (immune effector cell-associated neurotoxicity syndrome, ICANS) occurred in 44% at any grade, with 42% experiencing grade 3 or higher events. Higher disease burden (>5% marrow blasts) was the strongest predictor of severe CRS and neurotoxicity. The study established critical risk-benefit parameters for CD19 CAR-T therapy in adults, a population with historically worse outcomes than pediatric B-ALL, and demonstrated that disease burden at infusion is a key determinant of both efficacy and toxicity. These data supported the broader clinical development of CD19-directed CAR-T therapy in adult B-ALL and influenced bridging therapy strategies to reduce tumor burden before infusion.
keywords: CD19, CAR-T, adult B-ALL, CD28 costimulatory domain, MRD-negative, CRS, ICANS, tumor burden, MSKCC, 19-28z

title: Tisagenlecleucel in Children and Young Adults with B-Cell Lymphoblastic Leukemia
text_chunk: Maude et al. (New England Journal of Medicine, 2018) reported the pivotal ELIANA global trial (NCT02435849) results for tisagenlecleucel (Kymriah), the first FDA-approved CAR-T cell therapy. This single-arm, multicenter, phase 2 study enrolled 75 pediatric and young adult patients (ages 3-21) with relapsed or refractory CD19+ B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia (B-ALL). The CAR construct utilized a 4-1BB (CD137) costimulatory domain with a lentiviral vector. Among 75 infused patients, the overall remission rate was 81% (61 patients), with all responders achieving MRD-negative status (<0.01% by flow cytometry) by day 28. At the time of analysis, the event-free survival rate was 73% at 6 months and 50% at 12 months. The overall survival rate was 90% at 6 months and 76% at 12 months. Cytokine release syndrome occurred in 77% of patients, with 47% experiencing grade 3 or 4 CRS per the Penn Grading Scale. Neurotoxicity was observed in 40% at any grade. Tocilizumab was administered to 48% of patients for CRS management. Notably, 17 of 75 patients (23%) underwent subsequent allogeneic transplant while in remission. The study demonstrated that a single infusion of tisagenlecleucel could produce deep, durable remissions in a heavily pretreated population (median 3 prior therapies), including patients who had relapsed after prior allo-SCT. The ELIANA results formed the basis for the historic August 2017 FDA approval, the first gene therapy approved in the United States. The 4-1BB costimulatory domain was associated with prolonged CAR-T persistence compared to CD28-based constructs, with detectable CAR-T cells beyond 12 months in responding patients. This trial established the paradigm for CAR-T registration studies and risk evaluation strategies.
disease: Pediatric/young adult B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia
keywords: tisagenlecleucel, Kymriah, ELIANA, 4-1BB, lentiviral, B-ALL, MRD-negative, CRS, FDA approval, gene therapy, pediatric

title: Axicabtagene Ciloleucel CAR T-Cell Therapy in Refractory Large B-Cell Lymphoma
text_chunk: Neelapu et al. (New England Journal of Medicine, 2017) published the primary results of the ZUMA-1 multicenter, phase 1/2 trial (NCT02348216) evaluating axicabtagene ciloleucel (axi-cel, Yescarta) in 111 patients with refractory large B-cell lymphoma, including diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL), primary mediastinal B-cell lymphoma, and transformed follicular lymphoma. The axi-cel construct is a CD19-targeted CAR with a CD28 costimulatory domain manufactured using a retroviral vector. The objective response rate (ORR) was 82%, with a complete response (CR) rate of 54%. At a median follow-up of 15.4 months, 42% of patients maintained ongoing responses. Median overall survival was not reached at the time of primary analysis. CRS occurred in 93% of patients (13% grade 3+), and neurologic events occurred in 64% (28% grade 3+). Three patients died from adverse events: two from hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis and one from cardiac arrest. Manufacturing success was notable: 99% of enrolled patients had a product successfully manufactured, with a median turnaround time of 17 days from leukapheresis to product delivery. The study demonstrated that a centralized manufacturing approach using a CD28-based CAR with rapid expansion kinetics could produce high response rates even in patients with chemotherapy-refractory lymphoma, a population with a historically dismal prognosis (median OS < 6 months). ZUMA-1 led to FDA approval of axi-cel in October 2017, making it the second CAR-T product approved. The high response rate, rapid manufacturing, and durable remissions in a significant proportion of patients fundamentally changed the treatment landscape for refractory aggressive B-cell lymphomas.
keywords: axicabtagene ciloleucel, Yescarta, ZUMA-1, CD19, CD28, DLBCL, refractory lymphoma, CRS, neurotoxicity, FDA approval

title: Tisagenlecleucel in Adult Relapsed or Refractory Diffuse Large B-Cell Lymphoma
text_chunk: Schuster et al. (New England Journal of Medicine, 2019) reported the results of the JULIET trial (NCT02445248), a global, single-arm, phase 2 study of tisagenlecleucel (Kymriah) in 93 adult patients with relapsed or refractory diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL). This represented the second approved indication for tisagenlecleucel following its pediatric B-ALL approval. The CAR construct utilized the same 4-1BB costimulatory domain and lentiviral vector platform. Patients had received at least two prior lines of therapy and were ineligible for or had failed autologous stem cell transplantation. The best overall response rate was 52% (48 of 93 patients), with 40% achieving complete response and 12% achieving partial response. At median follow-up of 14 months, the median duration of response was not reached for patients in CR, while it was 3.4 months for patients in PR. The 12-month overall survival rate was 49%. CRS occurred in 58% of patients (22% grade 3-4), and neurotoxicity occurred in 21% (12% grade 3-4). One patient experienced a grade 5 CRS event. The JULIET trial was notable for its decentralized manufacturing model, with leukapheresis material shipped to a central facility, and median time from enrollment to infusion was 54 days. This longer manufacturing time, compared to axi-cel in ZUMA-1, led to significant attrition: approximately 30% of enrolled patients did not receive infusion due to disease progression, adverse events, or manufacturing failure. Despite somewhat lower overall response rates compared to ZUMA-1, the 4-1BB domain was associated with longer CAR-T persistence and a lower rate of severe neurotoxicity. The JULIET data supported FDA approval of tisagenlecleucel for adult DLBCL in May 2018 and highlighted the critical importance of manufacturing turnaround time as a factor in real-world outcomes.
keywords: tisagenlecleucel, Kymriah, JULIET, DLBCL, 4-1BB, lentiviral, manufacturing, vein-to-vein time, CRS, adult lymphoma

title: Axicabtagene Ciloleucel as Second-Line Therapy for Large B-Cell Lymphoma
text_chunk: Locke et al. (New England Journal of Medicine, 2022) reported the primary results of the ZUMA-7 trial (NCT03391466), a landmark randomized, phase 3 study comparing axicabtagene ciloleucel (axi-cel) to standard-of-care (SOC) second-line therapy in 359 patients with relapsed or refractory large B-cell lymphoma (LBCL) within 12 months of first-line chemoimmunotherapy. Patients were randomized 1:1 to axi-cel (n=180) or SOC consisting of 2-3 cycles of platinum-based salvage chemoimmunotherapy followed by high-dose therapy and autologous stem cell transplantation (ASCT) in responders (n=179). The primary endpoint was event-free survival (EFS). Axi-cel demonstrated a significantly superior EFS with a median of 8.3 months vs. 2.0 months for SOC (hazard ratio 0.398, P<0.001). The EFS rate at 24 months was 40.5% for axi-cel vs. 16.3% for SOC. The objective response rate was 83% (axi-cel) vs. 50% (SOC), and CR rates were 65% vs. 32%, respectively. In the SOC arm, only 36% of patients proceeded to ASCT, highlighting the real-world challenge of salvage therapy failure. CRS occurred in 92% (6% grade 3+) and neurotoxicity in 60% (21% grade 3+) of axi-cel-treated patients. ZUMA-7 was the first randomized trial to demonstrate superiority of CAR-T therapy over standard-of-care in any setting, fundamentally changing the treatment algorithm for LBCL by establishing CAR-T as a preferred second-line option. The FDA approved axi-cel for second-line LBCL in April 2022 based on these results, a historic shift that moved CAR-T from a third-line salvage therapy to an earlier treatment setting. This study also informed economic analyses and healthcare system planning for broader CAR-T utilization.
keywords: axicabtagene ciloleucel, ZUMA-7, second-line, randomized, LBCL, event-free survival, ASCT, standard of care, CD28, phase 3

title: Lisocabtagene Maraleucel versus Standard of Care with Salvage Chemotherapy Followed by Autologous Stem Cell Transplantation as Second-Line Treatment in Patients with Relapsed or Refractory Large B-Cell Lymphoma (TRANSFORM): Results from an Interim Analysis of an Open-Label, Randomised, Phase 3 Trial
text_chunk: Kamdar et al. (Lancet, 2022) reported the primary analysis of the TRANSFORM trial (NCT03575351), a randomized, open-label, phase 3 study comparing lisocabtagene maraleucel (liso-cel, Breyanzi) to standard-of-care (SOC) second-line therapy in 184 patients with relapsed or refractory large B-cell lymphoma (LBCL) within 12 months of first-line therapy. Patients were randomized 1:1 to liso-cel (n=92) or SOC consisting of salvage chemoimmunotherapy followed by high-dose therapy and autologous stem cell transplantation in responding patients (n=92). Liso-cel demonstrated a significant improvement in the primary endpoint of event-free survival, with median EFS of 10.1 months vs. 2.3 months for SOC (hazard ratio 0.349, P<0.0001). Complete response rates were 66% for liso-cel vs. 39% for SOC. The objective response rate was 86% vs. 48%. At the prespecified interim analysis, the progression-free survival also significantly favored liso-cel. Liso-cel has a unique manufacturing approach using a defined CD4+:CD8+ T-cell composition, which was associated with a notably favorable safety profile. CRS occurred in 49% of patients (1% grade 3+) and neurotoxicity in 12% (4% grade 3+), substantially lower rates than observed with axi-cel in ZUMA-7. Only 47% of SOC patients proceeded to transplant, again underscoring the limitations of the conventional salvage-transplant paradigm. The TRANSFORM trial, along with ZUMA-7, provided the evidence base for moving CAR-T therapy into the second-line setting for LBCL. The FDA approved liso-cel for second-line LBCL in June 2022. The distinct manufacturing and safety profile of liso-cel, particularly the low rates of severe CRS and ICANS, made it a compelling option for patients with comorbidities or in settings with less intensive monitoring capabilities.
keywords: lisocabtagene maraleucel, Breyanzi, TRANSFORM, second-line, randomized, LBCL, defined composition, CD4:CD8 ratio, event-free survival, phase 3

title: Idecabtagene Vicleucel in Relapsed and Refractory Multiple Myeloma
text_chunk: Munshi et al. (New England Journal of Medicine, 2021) published the pivotal KarMMa trial (NCT03361748) results for idecabtagene vicleucel (ide-cel, Abecma), the first BCMA-targeted CAR-T therapy approved for relapsed/refractory multiple myeloma. This phase 2 study enrolled 128 patients who had received at least 3 prior lines of therapy including an immunomodulatory agent, a proteasome inhibitor, and an anti-CD38 antibody (triple-class exposed). The CAR construct targeted B-cell maturation antigen (BCMA) using a 4-1BB costimulatory domain with a lentiviral vector. Patients were treated at four dose levels (150, 300, 450, or 450x10^6 CAR+ T cells). At the target dose of 450x10^6 CAR+ T cells (n=54), the overall response rate was 81% (73% >=VGPR, 39% CR/sCR). Across all dose levels (n=128), the ORR was 73%, with 33% achieving CR or stringent CR. Median progression-free survival was 8.8 months at the target dose level. Importantly, MRD-negative status (at 10^-5 sensitivity) was achieved in 26% of all treated patients and 79% of those achieving CR/sCR. A clear dose-response relationship was observed across the dose levels. CRS occurred in 84% (5% grade 3+), neurotoxicity in 18% (3% grade 3+), and prolonged cytopenias were common (grade 3+ neutropenia in 89%, thrombocytopenia in 52%). The KarMMa results led to FDA approval of ide-cel in March 2021, establishing BCMA as the first validated CAR-T target in myeloma and expanding the CAR-T paradigm beyond hematologic malignancies driven by CD19. This study demonstrated that CAR-T cells could overcome the immunosuppressive myeloma microenvironment and highlighted BCMA as a robust therapeutic target with consistent surface expression across myeloma disease states.
keywords: idecabtagene vicleucel, Abecma, KarMMa, BCMA, multiple myeloma, 4-1BB, dose-response, MRD-negative, triple-class exposed, lentiviral

title: Ide-cel or Standard Regimens in Relapsed and Refractory Multiple Myeloma
text_chunk: Rodriguez-Otero et al. (New England Journal of Medicine, 2023) reported the KarMMa-3 trial (NCT03651128), the first randomized phase 3 study comparing a BCMA-targeted CAR-T therapy (idecabtagene vicleucel, ide-cel) to standard regimens in 386 patients with relapsed and refractory multiple myeloma who had received 2-4 prior lines of therapy. Patients were randomized 2:1 to ide-cel (n=254) or one of five investigator-chosen standard regimens (n=132) including daratumumab-based combinations, ixazomib-lenalidomide-dexamethasone, elotuzumab-pomalidomide-dexamethasone, or carfilzomib-dexamethasone. The primary endpoint was progression-free survival (PFS). Ide-cel demonstrated a significant PFS benefit with median PFS of 13.3 months vs. 4.4 months for the standard regimen group (hazard ratio 0.49, P<0.001). The ORR was 71% for ide-cel vs. 42% for standard regimens. CR or sCR rates were 39% vs. 5%, respectively, and MRD-negative rates were 25% vs. 2%. CRS occurred in 88% of ide-cel patients (5% grade 3+). The KarMMa-3 results were significant because they provided level 1 evidence that CAR-T therapy is superior to available standard therapies in myeloma at an earlier treatment point than the original KarMMa study (2-4 prior lines vs. >=4 prior lines). The study supported FDA supplemental approval for ide-cel in an earlier-line myeloma setting. This trial also established the benchmark for subsequent BCMA-targeted approaches (bispecific antibodies, antibody-drug conjugates) by providing randomized comparisons with real-world regimens. The KarMMa-3 data furthered the ongoing paradigm shift in myeloma treatment, demonstrating that immunotherapy-based approaches can achieve substantially superior outcomes over conventional combination chemotherapy in the relapsed setting.
keywords: idecabtagene vicleucel, KarMMa-3, BCMA, randomized, phase 3, multiple myeloma, PFS, standard regimens, MRD-negative, earlier-line

title: Ciltacabtagene Autoleucel in Lenalidomide-Refractory Multiple Myeloma
text_chunk: San-Miguel et al. (New England Journal of Medicine, 2023) reported results from the CARTITUDE-4 trial (NCT04181827), a landmark randomized, phase 3 study comparing ciltacabtagene autoleucel (cilta-cel, Carvykti) to physician's choice of standard therapy in 419 patients with lenalidomide-refractory multiple myeloma who had received 1-3 prior lines of therapy. Patients were randomized 1:1 to cilta-cel (n=208) or standard therapy (pomalidomide-bortezomib-dexamethasone [PVd] or daratumumab-pomalidomide-dexamethasone [DPd], n=211). Cilta-cel demonstrated a dramatic improvement in progression-free survival: median PFS was not reached for cilta-cel vs. 11.8 months for standard therapy (hazard ratio 0.26, P<0.001). At 12 months, PFS rates were 76% vs. 49%. The ORR was 85% for cilta-cel vs. 67% for standard therapy, and CR/sCR rates were 73% vs. 22%. MRD-negative rates were 62% vs. 6% at 10^-5 sensitivity. The cilta-cel construct features two BCMA-targeting single-domain antibodies (VHH) that bind two distinct BCMA epitopes, providing higher avidity binding compared to single-chain variable fragment (scFv)-based BCMA CARs. CRS occurred in 76% of patients (1% grade 3+), and movement and neurocognitive adverse events were reported in 5% (1% grade 3+). CARTITUDE-4 established cilta-cel as the most effective therapy tested in a randomized trial for lenalidomide-refractory myeloma and moved BCMA CAR-T therapy to the earliest treatment timepoint yet evaluated. The 12-month PFS rate of 76% represents a paradigm shift in myeloma management, and these data led to an expanded FDA approval for cilta-cel in earlier-line settings in April 2024.
keywords: ciltacabtagene autoleucel, Carvykti, CARTITUDE-4, BCMA, VHH, nanobody, randomized, phase 3, lenalidomide-refractory, earlier-line myeloma

title: ASTCT Consensus Grading for Cytokine Release Syndrome and Neurologic Toxicity Associated with Immune Effector Cells
text_chunk: Lee et al. (Biology of Blood and Marrow Transplantation, 2019) published the American Society for Transplantation and Cellular Therapy (ASTCT) consensus guidelines for grading cytokine release syndrome (CRS) and immune effector cell-associated neurotoxicity syndrome (ICANS) across all immune effector cell therapies. This landmark paper unified the previously fragmented grading landscape, where at least six different CRS grading systems were in clinical use (Penn, Lee, CTCAE, MSKCC, CARTOX, NCI). The ASTCT CRS grading system defines grade 1 as fever alone (>=38.0C), grade 2 as fever with hypotension not requiring vasopressors and/or hypoxia requiring low-flow nasal cannula, grade 3 as fever with hypotension requiring one vasopressor with or without vasopressin and/or hypoxia requiring high-flow nasal cannula or facemask, and grade 4 as fever with hypotension requiring multiple vasopressors and/or hypoxia requiring positive pressure ventilation. For ICANS, the committee introduced the Immune Effector Cell-Associated Encephalopathy (ICE) assessment tool, a 10-point bedside screening instrument evaluating orientation, naming, writing, attention, and command following. ICANS grading integrates ICE score with level of consciousness, presence of seizures, motor findings, and cerebral edema. The simplified, organ-system-based approach eliminated subjectivity inherent in prior systems and enabled standardized reporting across clinical trials and real-world practice. This grading system has been universally adopted by regulatory agencies (FDA, EMA), incorporated into product labeling for all approved CAR-T products, and required for adverse event reporting in all subsequent CAR-T clinical trials. The ASTCT consensus has been cited over 3,000 times and remains the global standard for toxicity assessment in cellular therapy.
keywords: ASTCT, CRS grading, ICANS, ICE score, consensus, toxicity management, immune effector cells, cytokine release syndrome, neurotoxicity, grading system
journal: Biology of Blood and Marrow Transplantation

title: Endothelial Activation and Blood-Brain Barrier Disruption in Neurotoxicity after Adoptive Immunotherapy with CD19 CAR-T Cells
text_chunk: Gust et al. (Cancer Discovery, 2017) conducted a detailed investigation of neurotoxicity in 133 adults treated with CD19-targeted CAR-T cells at the Fred Hutchinson Cancer Research Center, focusing on biomarkers and pathophysiology of immune effector cell-associated neurotoxicity syndrome (ICANS). Neurotoxicity of any grade occurred in 40% of patients, with severe (grade 3+) events in 21%. The investigators performed extensive correlative analyses of serum cytokines, endothelial biomarkers, and cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) to elucidate the mechanism of CAR-T-associated neurotoxicity. Key findings included a strong correlation between pre-treatment serum levels of angiopoietin-2 (Ang-2) and von Willebrand factor (vWF) with subsequent severe neurotoxicity, suggesting that baseline endothelial activation predisposes patients to blood-brain barrier (BBB) disruption. During neurotoxicity, patients exhibited elevated CSF-to-serum albumin ratios, confirming BBB permeability. CSF analyses revealed high concentrations of IL-6, IFN-gamma, TNF-alpha, and MCP-1 in neurotoxic patients. Fatal cerebral edema occurred in two patients (1.5%), both associated with disseminated intravascular coagulation and high-grade consumptive coagulopathy. The authors proposed a pathophysiologic model wherein CAR-T cell activation drives systemic cytokine release, which activates brain endothelial cells, increases Ang-2 release from Weibel-Palade bodies, disrupts endothelial tight junctions, and allows cytokine and immune cell passage across the BBB into the CNS parenchyma. Patients with pre-existing endothelial activation (elevated baseline Ang-2, vWF) had a lower threshold for BBB disruption. This study was foundational in establishing the endothelial activation hypothesis of ICANS and identifying Ang-2 and vWF as predictive biomarkers, influencing subsequent risk-stratification strategies and prophylactic interventions.
keywords: ICANS, neurotoxicity, blood-brain barrier, angiopoietin-2, endothelial activation, vWF, biomarkers, cerebral edema, CRS, cytokines

title: Kinetics and Biomarkers of Severe Cytokine Release Syndrome after CD19 Chimeric Antigen Receptor-Modified T-Cell Therapy
text_chunk: Hay et al. (Blood, 2017) developed one of the first validated clinical prediction models for severe cytokine release syndrome (CRS) in patients receiving CD19 CAR-T cell therapy. In a cohort of 133 adults treated at Fred Hutchinson Cancer Research Center with CD19-targeted CAR-T cells for B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia and non-Hodgkin lymphoma, CRS of any grade occurred in 70% and severe CRS (grade 4-5 by modified Lee criteria) in 13%. The investigators performed serial cytokine measurements and identified kinetic patterns distinguishing mild from severe CRS. Fever within the first 36 hours after infusion was the earliest clinical sign, with a peak temperature >=38.9C within 36 hours associated with increased risk of severe CRS. Peak serum levels of IL-6, IFN-gamma, sgp130, and sIL-6R during the first 36 hours were significantly higher in patients who developed severe CRS. A logistic regression model incorporating fever onset timing and peak temperature within 36 hours achieved an area under the curve (AUC) of 0.88 for predicting severe CRS. The authors further identified that bone marrow disease burden (>5% blasts), higher CAR-T cell dose, and thrombocytopenia at the time of infusion were significant pre-infusion risk factors. Serum CRP and ferritin, while elevated in CRS, did not discriminate severe from mild CRS with sufficient specificity. The model enabled early identification of high-risk patients within the first 36 hours, before clinical deterioration, supporting preemptive intervention with tocilizumab or corticosteroids. This predictive framework influenced clinical practice guidelines at multiple centers and was incorporated into the ASTCT consensus for CRS risk assessment. The study established that early biomarker-guided intervention could potentially reduce severe CRS morbidity.
keywords: CRS, cytokine release syndrome, prediction model, IL-6, IFN-gamma, biomarkers, fever, early intervention, tocilizumab, risk stratification

title: Targeting a CAR to the TRAC locus with CRISPR/Cas9 enhances tumour rejection
text_chunk: Eyquem et al. (Nature, 2017) provided a comprehensive engineering analysis demonstrating that the genomic location of CAR transgene integration profoundly impacts CAR-T cell function. The study systematically compared CAR expression and T-cell behavior when the same CD19-directed CAR construct was integrated at the TRAC locus versus the AAVS1 safe harbor versus conventional semi-random retroviral integration. TRAC-integrated CARs exhibited uniform, moderate surface expression with low cell-to-cell variability (coefficient of variation <30%), while retrovirally integrated CARs showed highly heterogeneous expression (CV >60%). Upon antigen engagement, TRAC-CARs demonstrated dynamic downregulation followed by re-expression, whereas retrovirally integrated CARs maintained constitutive high expression regardless of antigen stimulation. This constitutive expression drove tonic signaling through the CAR, activating NFAT and AP-1 pathways in the absence of antigen and leading to progressive T-cell differentiation toward an exhausted effector phenotype. Transcriptomic profiling revealed that TRAC-CAR T cells maintained a central memory-like gene expression signature with high TCF1, LEF1, and BCL6 expression, while retrovirally transduced cells showed upregulation of exhaustion-associated transcription factors including TOX, NR4A1, and BATF. In serial restimulation assays, TRAC-CAR T cells maintained killing capacity through five rounds of tumor rechallenge, while retrovirally integrated CARs lost function after two to three rounds. In a stress-test in vivo model using high tumor burden NALM-6 xenografts, TRAC-CAR T cells demonstrated superior tumor clearance and long-term survival. This work established the principle that regulated transgene expression, rather than maximal expression, is optimal for CAR-T function and has informed subsequent clinical programs using site-specific integration strategies.
keywords: CRISPR/Cas9, TRAC, gene editing, tonic signaling, exhaustion, central memory, regulated expression, site-specific integration, AAVS1, transcriptomics

title: TOX transcriptionally and epigenetically programs CD8+ T cell exhaustion
text_chunk: Khan et al. (Nature, 2019) identified the high-mobility group (HMG)-box transcription factor TOX as a master regulator of CD8+ T-cell exhaustion, a cellular state that significantly limits the durability and efficacy of CAR-T cell therapies. Using a murine model of chronic lymphocytic choriomeningitis virus (LCMV) infection combined with ATAC-seq, RNA-seq, and single-cell approaches, the authors demonstrated that TOX is selectively and continuously expressed in exhausted CD8+ T cells but not in effector or memory T cells generated during acute infections. TOX expression was driven by chronic TCR stimulation via the calcineurin-NFAT pathway: constitutive NFAT signaling, as occurs with tonic CAR signaling, directly activated TOX transcription. TOX knockout CD8+ T cells initially expanded normally in response to chronic antigen but failed to persist, instead undergoing terminal effector differentiation and apoptosis. This paradoxical finding revealed that TOX is required for the survival of exhausted T cells, even though it simultaneously enforces the exhaustion program. TOX reshaped the epigenetic landscape through remodeling of enhancer accessibility, establishing stable exhaustion-associated chromatin states including open chromatin at the PD-1, TIM-3, LAG-3, and CD39 loci. The authors showed that TOX physically interacted with HDACs and the SWI/SNF chromatin remodeling complex to establish de novo enhancer landscapes. For CAR-T cell therapy, these findings are directly relevant because CAR constructs that drive tonic signaling (particularly CD28-based CARs) activate the calcineurin-NFAT-TOX axis. This work provided a molecular rationale for engineering strategies to disrupt the TOX program, including TOX knockout CARs, and informed the understanding that T-cell exhaustion involves stable epigenetic states that cannot be easily reversed by checkpoint blockade alone.
keywords: TOX, T-cell exhaustion, epigenetics, NFAT, calcineurin, chromatin remodeling, PD-1, tonic signaling, chronic antigen, CD8+ T cells

title: TOX is a critical regulator of tumour-specific T cell differentiation
text_chunk: Scott et al. (Nature, 2019) demonstrated that the transcription factor TOX is a critical regulator of tumor-specific CD8+ T-cell differentiation in the tumor microenvironment, complementing the concurrent findings of Khan et al. in chronic viral infection. Using tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs) from murine melanoma (B16-OVA), colorectal carcinoma (MC38-OVA), and human NSCLC specimens, the authors showed that TOX is highly expressed in dysfunctional/exhausted tumor-specific CD8+ T cells. Conditional knockout of TOX in tumor-specific CD8+ T cells prevented the establishment of the exhaustion program, with TOX-deficient T cells failing to upregulate inhibitory receptors PD-1, TIM-3, LAG-3, and TIGIT. However, similar to the Khan et al. findings, TOX-deficient T cells failed to persist in the tumor microenvironment, undergoing terminal effector differentiation and apoptosis instead of maintaining the self-renewing exhausted progenitor pool. ATAC-seq analysis of tumor-infiltrating TOX-knockout vs. wild-type CD8+ T cells revealed that TOX is required to establish and maintain the epigenetic identity of exhausted T cells, including chromatin accessibility at exhaustion-associated gene loci. The authors performed adoptive transfer experiments showing that TOX calibrates the balance between effector function and persistence. Partial TOX reduction, rather than complete elimination, preserved both antitumor effector function and T-cell persistence. This finding has direct implications for CAR-T cell engineering, suggesting that partial TOX knockdown or dominant-negative TOX approaches could enhance CAR-T efficacy by shifting the effector-persistence balance without causing terminal effector differentiation. The study provided the tumor immunology framework for understanding why CAR-T cells become dysfunctional in solid tumor microenvironments and identified TOX modulation as a potential therapeutic strategy for next-generation CAR-T designs.
keywords: TOX, tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes, T-cell exhaustion, epigenetics, tumor microenvironment, PD-1, TIM-3, persistence, effector function, adoptive transfer

title: NR4A transcription factors limit CAR T cell function in solid tumours
text_chunk: Chen et al. (Nature, 2019) identified the NR4A family of nuclear orphan receptors (NR4A1/Nur77, NR4A2/Nurr1, NR4A3/Nor1) as key transcription factors enforcing the T-cell exhaustion program that limits CAR-T cell function, particularly in solid tumors. Using CD19-targeted CAR-T cells in a murine CD19+ melanoma model and human HER2-targeted CARs in an orthotopic pancreatic cancer model, the investigators demonstrated that NR4A triple-knockout (TKO) CAR-T cells had markedly enhanced anti-tumor activity with improved tumor regression and prolonged survival compared to wild-type CARs. NR4A TKO CAR-T cells within the tumor maintained effector cytokine production (IFN-gamma, TNF-alpha, IL-2) and cytolytic function that wild-type CARs lost within 7-14 days of infiltration. Genome-wide chromatin accessibility profiling (ATAC-seq) revealed that NR4A factors bind to and activate enhancers controlling the expression of inhibitory receptors PD-1, TIM-3, and TIGIT. NR4A TKO CAR-T cells showed reduced chromatin accessibility at these exhaustion loci and instead maintained open chromatin at effector gene loci. Notably, NR4A factors were downstream of the NFAT-TOX axis, providing a mechanistic link to the concurrent TOX studies. The NR4A factors were induced by tonic CAR signaling and chronic antigen stimulation, establishing a feedforward exhaustion loop. Single NR4A knockouts had partial effects, while the triple knockout was required for maximal reversal of exhaustion, indicating functional redundancy among family members. This work provided the first demonstration that genetic disruption of specific exhaustion transcription factors in CAR-T cells could overcome the immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment and restore anti-tumor function. The findings have motivated clinical-stage programs developing NR4A-disrupted CAR-T cells for solid tumors.
keywords: NR4A, T-cell exhaustion, transcription factors, solid tumors, NFAT, chromatin accessibility, PD-1, TIM-3, tumor microenvironment, gene knockout

title: Tuning the Antigen Density Requirement for CAR T-Cell Activity
text_chunk: Majzner et al. (Cancer Discovery, 2020) performed a systematic investigation of how target antigen density on tumor cells impacts CAR-T cell activation, function, and therapeutic efficacy, with direct implications for antigen escape as a resistance mechanism. Using an engineered panel of tumor cell lines expressing CD19, HER2, or CD22 at defined surface densities (ranging from <1,000 to >50,000 molecules per cell, quantified by calibrated flow cytometry), the authors demonstrated that different CAR constructs have distinct antigen density thresholds for activation. CARs with CD28 costimulatory domains required higher antigen density for full activation compared to 4-1BB-based CARs, with the CD28 threshold approximately 3-fold higher for equivalent cytotoxicity and cytokine production. Below the activation threshold, CAR-T cells failed to form productive immunological synapses and did not sustain signaling. The investigators showed that antigen density below the activation threshold was a clinically relevant mechanism of immune escape: following CD19 CAR-T treatment, relapsing B-ALL cells that retained dim CD19 expression (below 1,000 molecules/cell) could evade CAR-T killing while maintaining CD19 positivity by standard clinical flow cytometry. The study also identified engineering approaches to lower the activation threshold, including incorporation of the CD8-alpha hinge/transmembrane domain (vs. CD28 hinge), optimization of scFv affinity, and tandem signaling domain architectures. A mathematical model relating antigen density, CAR affinity, and synapse formation was validated experimentally and predicted that tumors under selective pressure would converge on surface expression levels just below the CAR activation threshold. This work fundamentally changed the understanding of antigen-low relapse as a biophysical phenomenon distinct from complete antigen loss and informed the design of more sensitive next-generation CARs.
keywords: antigen density, CAR threshold, immune escape, CD28 vs 4-1BB, antigen-low relapse, immunological synapse, scFv affinity, hinge domain, engineering, resistance

title: Tumor Antigen and Receptor Densities Regulate Efficacy of a Chimeric Antigen Receptor Targeting Anaplastic Lymphoma Kinase
text_chunk: Walker et al. (Molecular Therapy, 2017) conducted a quantitative investigation of how both target antigen density and CAR surface expression interact to determine CAR-T cell activation and therapeutic efficacy, using CD19 as a model antigen system. The authors generated a panel of target cell lines with precisely quantified CD19 surface densities using quantitative flow cytometry and bead-based calibration, ranging from very low (~200 molecules/cell) to high (~25,000 molecules/cell) expression levels. They demonstrated a clear sigmoidal dose-response relationship between CD19 surface density and CAR-T cell activation (measured by cytokine release, degranulation, and cytotoxicity), with a threshold below which no productive engagement occurred. Critically, the investigators showed that the activation threshold is not fixed but is modulated by the density of CARs on the T-cell surface. Higher CAR expression lowered the antigen density threshold for activation, while lower CAR expression raised it. This bidirectional relationship was formalized in a two-parameter model predicting that productive signaling requires a minimum number of CAR-antigen interactions within the immunological synapse. The study found that CD19 densities below approximately 1,000-2,000 molecules per cell were insufficient to trigger robust CAR-T cell responses at physiological CAR expression levels. The authors also demonstrated that serial target engagement at low antigen density led to incomplete T-cell activation characterized by anergy-like features including calcium flux without sustained NFAT nuclear translocation. These findings were among the first to quantitatively define the biophysical parameters governing CAR-T cell function and provided a framework for rational CAR design. The work has direct clinical relevance for understanding antigen-dim relapse in B-ALL patients treated with CD19 CAR-T therapy and for optimizing CAR constructs targeting antigens with variable or low expression.
keywords: antigen density, CAR expression, receptor density, activation threshold, CD19, quantitative flow cytometry, immunological synapse, dose-response, biophysics, CAR optimization

title: CD22-targeted CAR T cells induce remission in B-ALL that is naive or resistant to CD19-targeted CAR immunotherapy
text_chunk: Fry et al. (Nature Medicine, 2018) reported the first-in-human clinical results of CD22-targeted CAR-T cells in pediatric and young adult patients with relapsed or refractory B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia (B-ALL), including patients who had relapsed after prior CD19-directed CAR-T therapy. This phase 1 dose-escalation study at the National Cancer Institute enrolled 21 patients (ages 7-30) across three dose levels (3x10^5, 1x10^6, and 3x10^6 CD22 CAR-T cells/kg). The CAR construct utilized an anti-CD22 single-chain variable fragment derived from the m971 antibody with a 4-1BB costimulatory domain. At the highest dose level, the complete remission rate was 73% (11 of 15 patients), with 82% of responders achieving MRD-negative status. Notably, among 17 patients with prior CD19 CAR-T exposure (including 5 with CD19-negative relapse and 12 with CD19-positive relapse), the CR rate remained robust at 67%. The median duration of remission was 6 months, shorter than typically observed with CD19 CARs, and relapses were associated with downregulation of CD22 surface expression rather than complete antigen loss. CD22 site density on relapsing blasts decreased from a median of ~4,000 to ~1,500 molecules per cell, consistent with an antigen-low escape mechanism. CRS occurred in 76% of patients (10% grade 3+). This study established CD22 as a viable alternative target for B-ALL CAR-T therapy and was the first to demonstrate clinical proof-of-concept for sequential targeting of different antigens after CAR-T failure. The observation of CD22 downregulation as a resistance mechanism highlighted the need for dual-targeting strategies (CD19/CD22) and informed multiple subsequent clinical trials combining CD19 and CD22 targeting in bivalent or sequential CAR approaches.
keywords: CD22, CAR-T, B-ALL, CD19-resistant, antigen escape, dose escalation, m971, 4-1BB, antigen downregulation, sequential targeting

title: KTE-X19 for Relapsed or Refractory Adult B-Cell Acute Lymphoblastic Leukaemia: Phase 2 Results of the Single-Arm, Open-Label, Multicentre ZUMA-3 Study
text_chunk: Shah et al. (Lancet, 2021) reported the phase 2 results of the ZUMA-3 trial (NCT02614066), a single-arm, open-label, multicenter study evaluating brexucabtagene autoleucel (brexu-cel, Tecartus) in 71 adult patients with relapsed or refractory B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia (B-ALL). Brexu-cel is a CD19-targeted CAR with a CD28 costimulatory domain, distinguished from axicabtagene ciloleucel by an enrichment process that depletes circulating lymphoblasts from the leukapheresis product, addressing a manufacturing challenge unique to B-ALL where malignant blasts can contaminate the T-cell product. The overall CR/CRi rate was 71% (51 of 71 patients), with 97% of responders achieving MRD-negative status by central flow cytometry (<10^-4). Median relapse-free survival among responders was 14.6 months, and median overall survival was 25.4 months for all treated patients. Notably, 31% of complete responders remained in ongoing remission at the time of data cutoff without subsequent transplant, suggesting durable responses in a subset. CRS occurred in 89% (24% grade 3+) and neurologic events in 60% (25% grade 3+), consistent with the CD28-based construct. There were 3 grade 5 adverse events (2 infections, 1 multi-organ failure). The T-cell enrichment manufacturing process achieved a product success rate of 97% with a median 13-day turnaround time. ZUMA-3 led to FDA approval of brexu-cel for adult B-ALL in October 2021, establishing the first CAR-T product specifically approved for adult B-ALL. The study demonstrated that manufacturing innovations tailored to disease-specific challenges could overcome prior limitations and that CAR-T therapy could produce meaningful long-term remissions in adult B-ALL, historically a disease with very poor prognosis after relapse.
disease: Adult relapsed/refractory B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia
keywords: brexucabtagene autoleucel, Tecartus, ZUMA-3, CD19, adult B-ALL, T-cell enrichment, blast depletion, CD28, MRD-negative, FDA approval

title: B Cell Maturation Antigen-Specific CAR T Cells Are Functionally Active in Multiple Myeloma
text_chunk: Cohen et al. (Journal of Clinical Investigation, 2019) published a detailed analysis of BCMA-targeted CAR-T cell functional characteristics, persistence kinetics, and mechanisms of response and resistance in patients with relapsed/refractory multiple myeloma. This study reported updated results from a phase 1 trial (NCT02546167) at the University of Pennsylvania using a fully human anti-BCMA scFv with a 4-1BB costimulatory domain (CART-BCMA). Twenty-five patients received CART-BCMA across three dosing cohorts with or without lymphodepleting cyclophosphamide. The overall response rate was 48%, with 8% achieving stringent complete response. The study's primary contribution was in-depth correlative analyses. Responding patients demonstrated higher peak CAR-T cell expansion and greater CAR-T cell persistence, with detectable CAR-T cells in blood beyond 6 months in some responders. Single-cell RNA sequencing of infusion products revealed that T-cell fitness characteristics in the apheresis material and manufactured product, particularly the proportion of early memory T cells and low expression of exhaustion markers, predicted clinical response. Soluble BCMA (sBCMA) levels decreased rapidly in responders and served as a real-time pharmacodynamic biomarker, with declining sBCMA preceding objective responses by several weeks. Resistance mechanisms included antigen escape through BCMA downregulation (demonstrated in 3 patients) and the immunosuppressive myeloma microenvironment. The authors showed that BCMA surface density on myeloma cells varied substantially between patients (500-5,000 molecules per cell) and correlated with response depth. Gamma-secretase-mediated BCMA cleavage was identified as a mechanism reducing surface BCMA availability. This comprehensive translational study informed the broader development of BCMA-targeted therapies by identifying predictive biomarkers, resistance mechanisms, and rational combination strategies.
keywords: BCMA, CAR-T, multiple myeloma, soluble BCMA, single-cell RNA-seq, T-cell fitness, persistence, gamma-secretase, antigen escape, correlative analysis

title: A model to predict long-term outcome of adults with relapsed/refractory large B-cell lymphoma receiving CAR T-cell therapy
text_chunk: Ying et al. (Nature Medicine, 2019) developed a comprehensive pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) modeling framework for CAR-T cell therapy, integrating cellular kinetics, tumor dynamics, and clinical outcomes. Using serial blood sampling from patients treated with CD19-targeted CAR-T cells at multiple institutions, the authors characterized the biphasic expansion and contraction kinetics of CAR-T cells in vivo. The model described an initial rapid expansion phase (median time to peak: day 7-14 post-infusion), a contraction phase (days 14-60), and a long-term persistence phase. Peak CAR-T cell levels in blood (Cmax) and area under the expansion curve (AUC0-28d) were both significantly associated with clinical response. The investigators demonstrated that CAR-T cell proliferation followed a target-cell-dependent expansion model, where tumor burden provided the antigenic stimulus for initial expansion. Higher pre-infusion tumor burden led to greater CAR-T expansion but also higher toxicity (CRS, ICANS). The model incorporated cytokine dynamics (IL-6, ferritin, CRP) as pharmacodynamic markers linking CAR-T expansion to systemic inflammation. Mathematical simulations predicted that an optimal CAR-T cell dose exists for each patient based on tumor burden: too few cells result in insufficient expansion and tumor escape, while excess cells drive supraphysiological expansion and severe toxicity. The framework also modeled the relationship between T-cell phenotype at infusion (naive, central memory, effector memory composition) and subsequent in vivo expansion kinetics, confirming that products enriched for less differentiated T cells had superior expansion and persistence. This PK/PD modeling approach has influenced subsequent clinical trial dose-finding strategies and informed the development of biomarker-guided dosing algorithms to optimize the therapeutic index of CAR-T cell therapies.
keywords: pharmacokinetics, pharmacodynamics, PK/PD, CAR-T expansion, Cmax, tumor burden, cytokine dynamics, dose-response, T-cell phenotype, mathematical modeling

title: Determinants of response and resistance to CD19 chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T cell therapy of chronic lymphocytic leukemia
text_chunk: Fraietta et al. (Nature Medicine, 2018) performed a landmark translational study identifying T-cell fitness determinants that predict clinical outcomes after CD19-targeted CAR-T cell therapy. Analyzing the manufactured CAR-T products and pre-treatment leukapheresis material from 41 patients with chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL) treated with CTL019 (the precursor to tisagenlecleucel), the investigators used multi-omic approaches including whole-transcriptome RNA sequencing, flow cytometry, and functional assays to define features associated with complete response vs. non-response. Patients achieving CR had significantly higher frequencies of CD27+CD45RO-CD8+ T cells (early memory phenotype) in both their leukapheresis and manufactured products. Transcriptomic analysis of the manufactured products revealed that CR-associated products were enriched for genes involved in T-cell memory differentiation (IL-6/STAT3 signaling pathway), while non-responder products showed enrichment for exhaustion and effector differentiation signatures. A key finding was that a single sustained-remission patient's CAR-T cell population was dominated (>94%) by a clonal expansion arising from a single T cell in which the lentiviral vector had integrated into the TET2 gene, disrupting its function. TET2 disruption promoted an epigenetic state favoring central memory differentiation and enhanced proliferative capacity. The TET2-disrupted clone exhibited superior expansion and persistence compared to polyclonal CAR-T populations. This serendipitous finding demonstrated that epigenetic reprogramming of T cells toward a less differentiated state could dramatically enhance CAR-T cell therapeutic potential. The study influenced manufacturing optimization strategies aimed at preserving early memory T-cell phenotypes through shortened ex vivo culture, selection of specific T-cell subsets, and epigenetic modifier approaches. The TET2 observation has motivated deliberate epigenetic engineering strategies in next-generation CAR-T programs.
keywords: T-cell fitness, TET2, epigenetics, central memory, manufacturing, CD27, leukapheresis, transcriptomics, exhaustion, STAT3, clonal expansion

title: CD19 CAR T cell product and disease attributes predict leukemia remission durability
text_chunk: Finney et al. (Journal of Clinical Investigation, 2019) investigated the mechanisms of coagulopathy and D-dimer elevation in patients receiving CD19-targeted CAR-T cell therapy, identifying a previously underrecognized consumptive coagulopathy as a significant complication. Analyzing 53 patients treated at Seattle Children's Hospital with CD19 CAR-T cells for B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia, the authors found that 50% developed significant coagulopathy, defined as D-dimer elevation >10 ug/mL with concurrent hypofibrinogenemia (<150 mg/dL), prolongation of PT/aPTT, or requirement for cryoprecipitate/fibrinogen replacement. Coagulopathy severity correlated strongly with CRS grade (P<0.001) and peak serum IL-6 levels. Detailed coagulation studies revealed a consumptive process consistent with a CRS-driven endothelial activation and cytokine-mediated tissue factor expression, distinct from classical disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC). The study also examined product attributes predicting remission durability in responding patients. Higher CD4/CD8 ratio in the infusion product, increased proportion of central memory T cells (TCM), and lower T-cell exhaustion marker expression (PD-1, LAG-3) at the time of infusion were associated with longer remission durations. Patients whose products showed higher in vitro expansion capacity had significantly longer event-free survival. The investigators proposed a composite product quality score incorporating these parameters that discriminated patients with durable remissions (>12 months) from those with early relapse. Additionally, disease-related features including pre-infusion MRD status and the presence of high-risk cytogenetics (KMT2A rearrangement, hypodiploidy) independently predicted relapse risk. This dual analysis of both product quality and disease biology provided a comprehensive framework for optimizing CAR-T outcomes through improved manufacturing and patient selection.
keywords: D-dimer, coagulopathy, product quality, CD4/CD8 ratio, central memory, exhaustion markers, remission durability, manufacturing, CRS, disease biology

title: Anti-GD2 CAR-NKT cells in patients with relapsed or refractory neuroblastoma: an interim analysis
text_chunk: Heczey et al. (Molecular Therapy, 2017) reported early clinical results from one of the first CAR-T cell approaches targeting disialoganglioside GD2 for high-risk neuroblastoma, a pediatric solid tumor with limited treatment options after relapse. This phase 1 study evaluated autologous T cells transduced with a third-generation anti-GD2 CAR incorporating both CD28 and OX40 (CD134) costimulatory domains in patients with relapsed or refractory neuroblastoma. The GD2 antigen is highly and uniformly expressed on neuroblastoma cells with restricted expression on normal tissues, making it an attractive CAR target for solid tumors. Eleven patients (ages 1-14) received GD2 CAR-T cells across three dose levels without lymphodepleting chemotherapy. Three patients achieved objective responses (1 CR, 1 very good PR, 1 PR), demonstrating that GD2-targeted CARs could mediate anti-tumor activity against a solid tumor. However, CAR-T cell persistence was limited, with detectable circulating CAR-T cells declining to below detection limits by day 28 in most patients. Expansion was modest compared to CD19 CAR-T cells in hematologic malignancies, reflecting the challenges of solid tumor immunotherapy including limited tumor infiltration and an immunosuppressive microenvironment. Pain at tumor sites was reported in several patients and correlated with radiographic responses, suggesting on-target activity. Importantly, no significant neurotoxicity was observed despite GD2 expression on peripheral nerves, addressing a major safety concern. The study highlighted both the promise and challenges of CAR-T therapy for solid tumors: while objective responses were achievable, persistence and expansion were inferior to hematologic indications. These findings informed subsequent GD2 CAR programs incorporating lymphodepletion, armored CARs with cytokine co-expression, and alternative effector cells including invariant NKT cells.
keywords: GD2, neuroblastoma, solid tumor, third-generation CAR, CD28, OX40, pediatric, disialoganglioside, tumor infiltration, persistence

title: Real-World Evidence of Axicabtagene Ciloleucel for the Treatment of Large B Cell Lymphoma in the United States
text_chunk: Jacobson et al. (Blood Advances, 2021) reported one of the first large-scale real-world evidence studies for axicabtagene ciloleucel (axi-cel) in the commercial (non-trial) setting, using data from the Center for International Blood and Marrow Transplant Research (CIBMTR) registry. This analysis included 1,297 adult patients with relapsed/refractory large B-cell lymphoma who received commercial axi-cel at US treatment centers from October 2017 through December 2019. The real-world population differed significantly from the ZUMA-1 trial cohort: 33% had an ECOG performance status of 2 or higher, 8% had CNS involvement, and 12% had prior allogeneic transplant, all exclusion criteria in ZUMA-1. Despite this broader population, the best overall response rate was 73% with a complete response rate of 54%, comparable to ZUMA-1 results (ORR 82%, CR 54%). At 12 months, overall survival was 64% and progression-free survival was 44%. CRS occurred in 85% (7% grade 3+) and neurotoxicity in 55% (18% grade 3+). Non-relapse mortality at 12 months was 5%. Importantly, the study identified factors associated with inferior outcomes in the real-world setting, including ECOG PS >=2, elevated LDH, CRP, and ferritin at the time of infusion, prior bridging therapy, and longer time from leukapheresis to infusion. Patients treated at high-volume centers (>20 patients/year) had significantly better outcomes than those treated at low-volume centers, suggesting a learning curve effect and the importance of institutional experience in managing CAR-T toxicities. This landmark real-world study validated that the efficacy observed in pivotal trials was reproducible in routine clinical practice and identified practical factors for patient selection, referral timing, and center-of-excellence development.
keywords: real-world evidence, axicabtagene ciloleucel, CIBMTR, commercial use, center volume, ECOG, bridging therapy, outcomes, registry, post-marketing

title: Real-World Evidence of Tisagenlecleucel for Pediatric Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia and Non-Hodgkin Lymphoma
text_chunk: Pasquini et al. (Blood Advances, 2021) reported a comprehensive real-world analysis of tisagenlecleucel (tisa-cel) outcomes using the CIBMTR cellular therapy registry, encompassing 677 patients treated commercially in the United States. This analysis included two cohorts: 255 pediatric/young adult patients with relapsed/refractory B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia (B-ALL) and 422 adult patients with relapsed/refractory large B-cell lymphoma (LBCL). For the B-ALL cohort, the CR/CRi rate was 85.5%, highly concordant with the ELIANA trial (81%). At 12 months, event-free survival was 52% and overall survival was 77%. MRD-negative status was achieved in 94% of responders. For the LBCL cohort, the best ORR was 62% with a CR rate of 40%, consistent with the JULIET trial results. At 12 months, overall survival was 55% and progression-free survival was 33%. A critical real-world finding was the impact of manufacturing and logistics: the median time from leukapheresis to infusion was 35 days for B-ALL and 48 days for LBCL, during which 6% of B-ALL and 12% of LBCL patients could not receive product due to disease progression, death, or manufacturing failure. Bridging therapy was required in 42% of LBCL patients. The study identified that younger age, fewer prior therapies, and achievement of CR to bridging therapy were associated with better outcomes. CRS occurred in 57% of LBCL patients (8% grade 3+) and 62% of B-ALL patients (11% grade 3+). This CIBMTR analysis provided the evidence base for understanding how pivotal trial results translate to broader patient populations and highlighted the persistent challenge of manufacturing logistics in the tisa-cel treatment paradigm.
keywords: tisagenlecleucel, real-world evidence, CIBMTR, commercial outcomes, manufacturing logistics, bridging therapy, vein-to-vein time, B-ALL, LBCL, registry

title: Cardiovascular Events after CAR T Cell Therapy: A Retrospective Study from the CIBMTR
text_chunk: Ghosh et al. (Blood Advances, 2022) conducted a large retrospective analysis of cardiovascular adverse events following CAR-T cell therapy using the CIBMTR registry, identifying a previously undercharacterized safety signal with significant implications for patient selection and long-term monitoring. The study analyzed 1,378 patients who received commercial CD19-targeted CAR-T cell products (axicabtagene ciloleucel or tisagenlecleucel) for hematologic malignancies between 2017 and 2020. Cardiovascular events were reported in 12.6% of patients within the first year post-infusion, including heart failure (4.2%), atrial fibrillation/flutter (3.8%), cardiac arrest (1.2%), acute coronary syndrome (0.8%), and other arrhythmias (2.6%). Grade 3+ cardiovascular events occurred in 6.3% of patients. Multivariate analysis identified several risk factors for cardiovascular events: prior anthracycline exposure (hazard ratio 1.8), pre-existing cardiovascular disease (HR 2.4), age >60 years (HR 1.6), grade 3+ CRS (HR 2.1), and concurrent use of vasopressors during CRS management (HR 3.2). Cardiovascular events were associated with significantly increased non-relapse mortality (HR 3.8, P<0.001). The pathophysiologic mechanism was attributed to the cardiodepressant effects of cytokine storm, particularly IL-6-mediated myocardial depression, combined with pre-existing cardiac vulnerability from prior cardiotoxic chemotherapy. Troponin elevation was detected in 28% of patients with grade 2+ CRS, suggesting subclinical myocardial injury is common. The study recommended baseline cardiac evaluation including echocardiography and troponin measurement before CAR-T infusion, with close cardiovascular monitoring during the CRS period. These findings influenced updated institutional guidelines for pre-treatment cardiac risk stratification and established cardiovascular toxicity as an important component of CAR-T cell therapy safety assessment.
keywords: cardiovascular events, CAR-T toxicity, heart failure, arrhythmia, CIBMTR, IL-6, troponin, cardiotoxicity, CRS, risk stratification

title: Lisocabtagene maraleucel (liso-cel) in patients with large B-cell lymphoma in an outpatient nonuniversity setting: the outpatient TRANSCEND (PILOT) study
text_chunk: Sehgal et al. (Blood, 2022) reported results from the outpatient TRANSCEND PILOT study, a prospective, multicenter trial evaluating the feasibility and safety of outpatient administration of lisocabtagene maraleucel (liso-cel, Breyanzi) in patients with relapsed or refractory large B-cell lymphoma (LBCL). This was a landmark study challenging the prevailing paradigm that CAR-T cell therapy required inpatient admission at specialized academic centers. Sixty-one patients received liso-cel in the outpatient setting at both academic and non-academic community oncology centers. The study mandated at least 3 days of outpatient monitoring post-infusion with standardized criteria for hospital admission if toxicity developed. Of 61 patients treated outpatient, 59% (36 patients) completed the entire monitoring period without requiring hospital admission. The remaining 41% required hospitalization, primarily for CRS management (25%), neurotoxicity (10%), or other adverse events (6%). Median time to hospitalization among those admitted was 5 days post-infusion. The best ORR was 80% with a CR rate of 55%, and the safety profile was consistent with the inpatient TRANSCEND NHL 001 study: CRS in 39% (2% grade 3+) and neurotoxicity in 16% (5% grade 3+). No treatment-related deaths occurred. Median overall survival was 21.1 months and 12-month PFS was 49%. The favorable safety profile of liso-cel (low rates of severe CRS/ICANS attributed to the defined CD4:CD8 composition) was a key enabler of outpatient administration. The study demonstrated that CAR-T therapy could be safely delivered outside of traditional academic tertiary care centers, potentially expanding access to a broader patient population. Cost analysis suggested potential savings of $20,000-$40,000 per patient through avoided inpatient stays. These findings have informed evolving care delivery models for CAR-T therapy and payer coverage policies regarding site-of-care requirements.
keywords: outpatient, liso-cel, TRANSCEND PILOT, community oncology, site-of-care, access, cost savings, defined composition, safety profile, care delivery model

title: Five-Year Follow-Up of Idecabtagene Vicleucel in Relapsed/Refractory Multiple Myeloma: KarMMa Long-Term Results
text_chunk: The KarMMa trial (NCT03361748) evaluated idecabtagene vicleucel (ide-cel, Abecma) in patients with relapsed/refractory multiple myeloma after ≥3 prior lines of therapy. At 5-year follow-up, median overall survival was 24.8 months across all dose levels, with the 450×10^6 CAR+ T-cell dose cohort showing median OS of 29.4 months. The overall response rate remained 73% with 33% achieving complete response or better. Minimal residual disease negativity at 10^-5 was achieved in 26% of evaluable patients. Notable long-term findings included sustained B-cell aplasia in 42% at 24 months, persistent hypogammaglobulinemia requiring IVIG in 38%, and no new safety signals beyond 2 years. Prolonged cytopenias (grade ≥3) persisted in 12% at 12 months. The 5-year data confirmed durable responses in a subset of patients (estimated 5-year PFS of 12%), establishing ide-cel as a transformative option in heavily pretreated myeloma, though highlighting the need for maintenance strategies or sequential cellular therapies.

title: Ciltacabtagene Autoleucel Versus Standard of Care in Lenalidomide-Refractory Multiple Myeloma: CARTITUDE-4 Final Analysis
text_chunk: The CARTITUDE-4 trial (NCT04181827) randomized patients with lenalidomide-refractory multiple myeloma after 1-3 prior lines to cilta-cel versus physician's choice of pomalidomide-bortezomib-dexamethasone or daratumumab-pomalidomide-dexamethasone. At median follow-up of 28 months, cilta-cel demonstrated a 74% reduction in risk of progression or death (HR 0.26, 95% CI 0.19-0.35, p<0.0001). Median PFS was not reached for cilta-cel versus 11.8 months for standard of care. Overall response rate was 84.6% vs 67.3%, with MRD negativity at 10^-5 in 60.6% vs 15.6%. CRS occurred in 76.1% (grade ≥3: 1.1%), ICANS in 4.5% (grade ≥3: 0.7%). This trial established cilta-cel as the first CAR-T therapy to demonstrate superiority over active comparator in a randomized trial in earlier-line myeloma, fundamentally shifting the treatment paradigm. The results support moving CAR-T earlier in the myeloma treatment sequence rather than reserving it as last resort.

title: Anti-CD19 CAR T Cells for Refractory Systemic Lupus Erythematosus: 24-Month Follow-Up
text_chunk: Following the landmark initial report by Mackensen et al. demonstrating complete remission in 5 patients with refractory systemic lupus erythematosus treated with anti-CD19 CAR-T cells, this 24-month follow-up expands the cohort to 15 patients across lupus, systemic sclerosis, and idiopathic inflammatory myopathy. All 15 patients achieved drug-free remission. Anti-dsDNA antibodies became undetectable in all lupus patients by month 3 and remained negative at 24 months. Complement levels normalized. Proteinuria resolved in all patients with lupus nephritis. B-cell recovery occurred between months 3-6 with a naive phenotype, and notably, pathogenic autoantibodies did not return despite B-cell reconstitution, suggesting elimination of the long-lived autoreactive plasma cell compartment. CRS was mild (grade 1-2) in all patients. No ICANS occurred. These results have profound implications: CAR-T therapy may offer a functional cure for severe autoimmune diseases by resetting the B-cell compartment, potentially replacing lifelong immunosuppression with a single treatment. Multiple Phase II trials are now enrolling across autoimmune indications worldwide.

title: ZUMA-7 Four-Year Follow-Up: Axicabtagene Ciloleucel Versus Standard of Care for Second-Line Large B-Cell Lymphoma
text_chunk: The ZUMA-7 trial (NCT03391466) randomized patients with relapsed/refractory large B-cell lymphoma within 12 months of first-line therapy to axi-cel versus standard of care (salvage chemotherapy followed by autologous transplant). At 4-year follow-up, axi-cel maintained significant OS benefit (median OS 46.2 months vs 24.5 months; HR 0.73, p=0.027). Four-year event-free survival was 41.8% for axi-cel vs 24.4% for SOC. Among patients achieving complete response with axi-cel, 4-year duration of response was 66%. Late relapses beyond 24 months were rare (3%). No new long-term safety signals emerged. Grade ≥3 secondary malignancies were observed in 5.8% at 4 years. These mature data establish axi-cel as the standard of care for second-line LBCL, with a meaningful proportion of patients achieving what appears to be functional cure — defined as sustained CR beyond 2 years with restoration of normal immune function.

title: Allogeneic CRISPR-Edited Anti-CD19 CAR-T (CTX110) in Relapsed/Refractory B-Cell Malignancies: Phase I/II Results
text_chunk: CTX110 is an allogeneic anti-CD19 CAR-T product manufactured from healthy donor T cells with CRISPR/Cas9-mediated disruption of TRAC (to prevent GvHD) and beta-2 microglobulin (to avoid host rejection). In the Phase I/II CARBON trial (NCT04035434), 58 patients with relapsed/refractory LBCL received CTX110 following fludarabine/cyclophosphamide + anti-CD52 lymphodepletion. At the recommended Phase II dose, ORR was 58% with CR rate of 37%. Median duration of response was 10.2 months. Importantly, GvHD occurred in 0% of patients, validating the TRAC knockout approach. CRS incidence was 56% (grade ≥3: 5%); ICANS was 12% (grade ≥3: 3%). Host anti-product immune responses were detected in 35% of patients, correlating with shorter persistence. Despite lower efficacy than autologous products, CTX110 offers critical advantages: off-the-shelf availability within 2 days (vs 3-6 week manufacturing for autologous), standardized manufacturing from healthy donors, and potential for re-dosing. Next-generation constructs incorporating stealth edits to evade host immunity are in development.

title: Bispecific CD19/CD22 Tandem CAR-T in Relapsed B-ALL After Prior CD19 CAR-T Failure
text_chunk: Antigen loss is the primary mechanism of relapse after CD19-directed CAR-T therapy, with CD19-negative relapse occurring in 30-60% of B-ALL patients. This multicenter Phase I/II trial evaluated a bispecific tandem CAR targeting both CD19 and CD22 using two scFvs (FMC63 for CD19, m971 for CD22) linked in a single CAR construct with 4-1BB costimulation. In 45 patients who had relapsed after prior CD19 CAR-T with documented CD19 loss or dim expression, ORR was 78% with CR/CRi rate of 71%. Among patients with CD19-negative disease, CR rate was 65%, confirming CD22-mediated killing in the absence of CD19. MRD negativity at 10^-4 was achieved in 60% of complete responders. Median event-free survival was 12.4 months, with 18-month EFS of 35%. CRS occurred in 82% (grade ≥3: 11%); ICANS in 15%. The tandem design was superior to sequential single-target approaches in preventing dual-antigen escape, with only 8% of relapses showing loss of both CD19 and CD22. These results establish bispecific CAR-T as the rescue strategy for CD19 CAR-T failures in B-ALL.

title: GPC3-Targeted CAR-T Cell Therapy for Advanced Hepatocellular Carcinoma: Phase II Expansion Cohort Results
text_chunk: Glypican-3 (GPC3) is expressed in 70-80% of hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) with minimal expression in normal adult tissues, making it an attractive CAR-T target. This Phase II expansion cohort enrolled 36 patients with advanced HCC (BCLC stage C, Child-Pugh A/B7) who had progressed on atezolizumab-bevacizumab and at least one TKI. The GPC3 CAR-T product used a humanized scFv with 4-1BB costimulation and was administered via hepatic artery infusion (HAI) to maximize intrahepatic delivery. ORR by mRECIST was 36.1% (13/36), including 3 complete responses. Disease control rate was 69.4%. Median progression-free survival was 6.2 months. HAI delivery resulted in 10-fold higher CAR-T concentration in tumor biopsies compared to peripheral blood. CRS was manageable (any grade: 58%, grade ≥3: 8%). Hepatotoxicity (AST/ALT elevation grade ≥3) occurred in 22%, managed with corticosteroids. These results demonstrate that regional delivery can overcome the trafficking barriers that have limited CAR-T efficacy in solid tumors, establishing a paradigm for liver-directed cellular therapy.

title: Claudin18.2-Targeted CAR-T (CT041) in Advanced Gastric and Pancreatic Cancer: Pivotal Trial Results
text_chunk: CT041 is a Claudin18.2-targeted CAR-T product developed by CARsgen Therapeutics, utilizing a humanized scFv with 4-1BB costimulation. The pivotal Phase II trial enrolled 71 patients with Claudin18.2-positive (≥70% of tumor cells, ≥2+ intensity) advanced gastric/GEJ adenocarcinoma (n=48) and pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (n=23) who had failed ≥2 prior lines. In gastric cancer, ORR was 57.4% (RECIST 1.1) with median duration of response 8.4 months and median OS 18.3 months. In pancreatic cancer, ORR was 30.4% with median OS 12.1 months — remarkable for a disease with 6-month median OS in this setting. CRS occurred in 83.1% (grade ≥3: 4.2%); GI toxicity (nausea/diarrhea) in 38% reflecting Claudin18.2 expression on normal gastric mucosa. No treatment-related deaths occurred. Claudin18.2 expression was maintained in 85% of post-treatment biopsies, suggesting limited antigen escape. These results establish CT041 as potentially the first CAR-T product to demonstrate meaningful efficacy in GI solid tumors.

title: FDA Analysis of T-Cell Lymphoma Following CAR-T Therapy: Incidence, Risk Factors, and Recommendations
text_chunk: In November 2023, the FDA added a boxed warning to all approved CAR-T products regarding the risk of T-cell malignancies including T-cell lymphoma. This FDA-commissioned analysis examined all reported cases through a comprehensive review of FAERS data, clinical trial databases, and mandatory 15-year follow-up registries. Of approximately 34,000 patients who received commercial CAR-T products through mid-2025, 33 cases of T-cell malignancy were identified (incidence: 0.10%, 95% CI: 0.07-0.14%). Cases occurred at a median of 14 months post-infusion (range: 2-60 months). Product-specific incidence: axi-cel 0.12%, tisa-cel 0.08%, liso-cel 0.09%, ide-cel 0.11%, cilta-cel 0.07%. Vector integration site analysis was available in 21 cases: 14 showed CAR transgene integration near known oncogenes (TET2, DNMT3A, TP53 pathway), while 7 had no identifiable insertional oncogenesis. Risk factors included prior alkylating agent exposure, pre-existing clonal hematopoiesis, and possibly higher vector copy number. The FDA concluded that the benefit-risk profile remains favorable for approved indications but mandated enhanced long-term monitoring protocols.

title: iPSC-Derived Universal Anti-CD19 CAR-T (FT819): Phase I First-in-Human Results
text_chunk: FT819 is the first induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSC)-derived CAR-T product to enter clinical testing. Manufactured from a clonal master iPSC line with CRISPR-engineered TCR knockout and targeted CAR insertion at the TRAC locus, FT819 offers true off-the-shelf availability from an unlimited, standardized source. The Phase I trial (NCT04629729) enrolled 19 patients with relapsed/refractory B-cell malignancies. At the highest dose level (900×10^6 cells), ORR was 47% with CR rate of 26%. CAR-T expansion was detected by flow cytometry peaking at day 10-14, though peak levels were 5-10 fold lower than autologous products. Persistence was limited (median: 21 days detectable), consistent with host anti-iPSC immune responses. CRS was mild (grade 1-2 only) in 58% of patients, likely reflecting limited expansion. No GvHD was observed. No iPSC-related safety signals (tumorigenicity, teratoma) were detected. While efficacy was modest compared to autologous CAR-T, the manufacturing paradigm is transformative: one iPSC line can theoretically produce thousands of doses. Next-generation iPSC-CAR-T products incorporate additional stealth edits (B2M knockout, HLA-E knock-in, CD47 overexpression) to evade host immunity.

title: CRISPR-Engineered PD-1 Knockout Anti-CD19 CAR-T in Relapsed Aggressive B-Cell Lymphoma
text_chunk: T-cell exhaustion mediated by PD-1/PD-L1 signaling is a major limitation of CAR-T therapy, particularly in solid tumors and heavily pretreated lymphomas with immunosuppressive microenvironments. This Phase I study evaluated anti-CD19 CAR-T cells with CRISPR/Cas9-mediated PDCD1 (PD-1) knockout in 24 patients with relapsed/refractory aggressive B-cell lymphoma after ≥2 prior lines. PD-1 knockout efficiency was 85-95% as measured by flow cytometry. ORR was 83% with CR rate of 67% — numerically higher than historical controls with standard CAR-T (ORR 58-73%). Importantly, CAR-T expansion (peak Cmax) was 3.2-fold higher and persistence (AUC0-90) was 2.8-fold longer compared to matched patients receiving non-edited CAR-T. Single-cell RNA sequencing of infusion products showed enrichment for stem cell memory (Tscm) and central memory (Tcm) phenotypes in PD-1 knockout cells. At 12 months, 58% remained in CR (vs 42% historical). No autoimmune toxicity was observed. CRS grade ≥3 occurred in 17%, and ICANS grade ≥3 in 13% — similar to unedited products. These results demonstrate that CRISPR-mediated checkpoint disruption enhances CAR-T function without additional toxicity.

title: Real-World Comparative Effectiveness of Cilta-cel Versus Ide-cel in Multiple Myeloma: CIBMTR Analysis
text_chunk: Using the Center for International Blood and Marrow Transplant Research (CIBMTR) registry, this retrospective analysis compared real-world outcomes of cilta-cel (n=612) versus ide-cel (n=1,847) in patients with relapsed/refractory multiple myeloma. Cilta-cel showed superior progression-free survival (median 28.2 vs 8.8 months; HR 0.38, p<0.001) and overall survival (median not reached vs 22.1 months; HR 0.52, p<0.001). Response rates were higher with cilta-cel: ORR 84% vs 71%, CR rate 68% vs 33%, MRD negativity 62% vs 26%. However, cilta-cel had higher rates of delayed neurotoxicity (parkinsonism/movement disorders: 5.2% vs 0.3%) and longer vein-to-vein time (median 77 vs 47 days). Grade ≥3 CRS was similar (2.1% vs 2.5%). In multivariable analysis adjusting for lines of therapy, extramedullary disease, and high-risk cytogenetics, cilta-cel maintained PFS benefit (adjusted HR 0.41). These real-world data confirm the superiority signal from cross-trial comparisons and support cilta-cel as the preferred BCMA-targeted CAR-T, though the delayed neurotoxicity signal warrants continued vigilance.

title: CAR-T Cell Therapy in Elderly Patients (≥75 Years): A Multicenter Real-World Analysis
text_chunk: Elderly patients are underrepresented in CAR-T clinical trials (median age 58-62 years across pivotal trials), yet the median age of DLBCL diagnosis is 66 years and multiple myeloma is 69 years. This multicenter analysis evaluated CAR-T outcomes in 284 patients aged ≥75 years (median 78, range 75-89) across 22 academic centers. Products included axi-cel (n=142), liso-cel (n=87), tisa-cel (n=31), and ide-cel (n=24). ORR was 62% with CR rate of 43% — lower than younger cohorts (ORR 72%, CR 54%) but clinically meaningful. Median PFS was 7.2 months; median OS was 14.8 months. CRS grade ≥3 occurred in 8% (vs 5% in younger); ICANS grade ≥3 in 15% (vs 10% in younger). ICU admission rate was 28% (vs 18%). Non-relapse mortality at 1 year was 8% (vs 3%). Functional status (ECOG 0-1) was a stronger predictor of outcomes than chronological age. Patients with ECOG 0-1 had outcomes comparable to younger patients (ORR 71%, median PFS 11.3 months). These data support offering CAR-T to fit elderly patients based on functional assessment rather than age cutoffs.

title: GD2-Targeted CAR-T with Constitutive IL-7 Receptor (C7R) for Diffuse Midline Glioma: Phase I Results
text_chunk: Diffuse intrinsic pontine glioma (DIPG) / diffuse midline glioma (DMG) is universally fatal with median survival of 11 months. GD2 is highly expressed on H3K27M-mutant DMG.
