This Phase I trial (NCT05298995) evaluated GD2-CAR-T cells engineered with a constitutive IL-7 receptor (C7R) that provides tonic cytokine signaling independent of the TME, designed to overcome the immunosuppressive CNS microenvironment. Twelve patients aged 4-23 years received intracerebroventricular (ICV) delivery of C7R-GD2-CAR-T. Dose-limiting toxicity (cerebral edema) defined the maximum tolerated dose. Of 10 evaluable patients, 4 (40%) showed radiographic response (partial response or better by RANO criteria). Three patients had sustained responses >6 months. CSF cytokine analysis confirmed robust CAR-T activation within the CNS compartment. No systemic CRS occurred, though CNS inflammation (headache, fever, transient neurologic worsening) was universal. These early results provide proof-of-concept that armored CAR-T cells with regional delivery can achieve responses in the most challenging solid tumor — pediatric CNS malignancies.

title: Mesothelin-Targeted CAR-T with Dominant-Negative TGF-beta Receptor in Malignant Pleural Mesothelioma
text_chunk: The tumor microenvironment of mesothelioma is highly immunosuppressive, with abundant TGF-beta production that drives T-cell exhaustion and regulatory T-cell recruitment. This Phase I trial evaluated mesothelin-targeted CAR-T cells co-expressing a dominant-negative TGF-beta receptor II (dnTGFbRII) that renders the CAR-T cells resistant to TGF-beta-mediated suppression. Twenty-one patients with unresectable malignant pleural mesothelioma received intrapleural CAR-T delivery following cyclophosphamide conditioning. ORR by modified RECIST was 43% (9/21), including 2 complete metabolic responses on PET-CT. Median PFS was 5.8 months with 3 patients progression-free beyond 12 months. Tumor biopsies in responding patients showed dense CAR-T infiltration with retained effector function (granzyme B+, IFN-gamma+) despite high TGF-beta levels, confirming dnTGFbRII function in vivo. Pleural CRS (pleuritis, effusion) occurred in 76% and was manageable with standard therapy. Systemic CRS was mild (grade 1-2 in 52%). These results validate the armored CAR-T approach for overcoming TME-mediated resistance in solid tumors.

title: NKG2D-Based CAR-T (CYAD-101) in Metastatic Colorectal Cancer: Phase Ib Dose-Escalation Results
text_chunk: CYAD-101 is an allogeneic NKG2D-based CAR-T product developed by Celyad Oncology, incorporating a non-gene-edited approach using a TCR inhibitory molecule (TIM) peptide to prevent GvHD. NKG2D ligands (MICA, MICB, ULBP1-6) are stress-induced surface molecules upregulated on cancer cells but minimally expressed on healthy tissue. In this Phase Ib trial (NCT04167696), 36 patients with microsatellite-stable metastatic colorectal cancer received CYAD-101 in combination with standard FOLFOX chemotherapy. Disease control rate was 72% (26/36) with 3 confirmed partial responses (8.3% ORR). Median PFS was 5.9 months — numerically superior to historical FOLFOX alone (4.5 months) in this heavily pretreated population. No GvHD was observed, validating the TIM approach. CRS was mild (grade 1-2 in 44%, no grade ≥3). Correlative studies showed NKG2D ligand upregulation on tumor cells following FOLFOX, suggesting chemotherapy-induced stress sensitizes tumors to NKG2D-CAR-T killing — a synergistic mechanism supporting combination approaches.

title: B7-H3-Targeted CAR-T Administered Intracranially for Recurrent Pediatric CNS Tumors
text_chunk: B7-H3 (CD276) is overexpressed in >90% of pediatric CNS tumors including DIPG/DMG, ependymoma, and medulloblastoma, with minimal expression in normal brain parenchyma. This Phase I trial (NCT04483778) at Seattle Children's evaluated intracerebroventricular (ICV) administration of B7-H3-CAR-T in 18 patients with recurrent/progressive CNS tumors. Diagnoses included DIPG/DMG (n=8), ependymoma (n=5), and medulloblastoma (n=5). Repeated ICV dosing (up to 4 infusions) was feasible. Radiographic responses were observed in 6/18 patients (33%): 2 CR and 4 PR by RANO criteria. All responses occurred in patients receiving ≥2 infusions. CNS inflammation (headache, fever, CSF pleocytosis) was universal and manageable. No systemic CRS or ICANS occurred. The compartmentalized delivery resulted in high CAR-T concentration in CSF (100-1000x peripheral blood). These results establish intracranial B7-H3-CAR-T as a promising approach for pediatric CNS tumors, a population with extremely limited treatment options after standard therapy failure.

title: Health Equity in CAR-T Cell Therapy: Racial Disparities in Access, Utilization, and Outcomes
text_chunk: Despite the transformative efficacy of CAR-T therapy, significant disparities exist in who receives treatment. This population-based analysis using linked SEER-Medicare and commercial claims data examined CAR-T utilization patterns among 8,247 patients with relapsed/refractory DLBCL or multiple myeloma who met clinical eligibility criteria between 2021-2024. Overall, only 12.3% of eligible patients received CAR-T. Disparities were stark: CAR-T utilization was 15.8% in White patients, 8.2% in Black patients, 7.1% in Hispanic patients, and 9.4% in Asian patients (p<0.001). After adjustment for insurance, comorbidity, and disease characteristics, Black patients were 42% less likely to receive CAR-T (adjusted OR 0.58, 95% CI 0.49-0.69). Key barriers included distance to CAR-T center (median 78 miles for rural patients vs 12 miles urban), lack of caregiver support, insurance authorization delays (median 21 days for Medicaid vs 8 days for commercial), and implicit referral bias. Among patients who did receive CAR-T, outcomes were similar across racial groups (ORR: White 71%, Black 69%, Hispanic 72%), indicating biological equivalence when access barriers are overcome.

title: CD19 CAR-T for Refractory Idiopathic Inflammatory Myopathy and Systemic Sclerosis: Expanding Autoimmune Indications
text_chunk: Building on the success of CAR-T in lupus, this multicenter study evaluated anti-CD19 CAR-T in 12 patients with refractory autoimmune diseases beyond SLE: 6 with idiopathic inflammatory myopathy (IIM, including anti-synthetase syndrome and dermatomyositis) and 6 with diffuse cutaneous systemic sclerosis (dcSSc). All patients had failed ≥3 immunosuppressive agents. In IIM patients, CK levels normalized in 5/6 by month 3, muscle strength improved by ≥1 MRC grade in all, and lung function (FVC) improved by mean 12% at 6 months. In dcSSc patients, modified Rodnan skin score decreased by mean 14 points (from 28 to 14) at 6 months, and FVC improved by mean 8%. No IIM or dcSSc patient required restart of immunosuppression through 12-month follow-up. B-cell depletion was complete by day 14 in all patients, with reconstitution beginning at months 3-5. CRS was mild (grade 1-2 in 83%, no grade ≥3). These results expand the CAR-T autoimmune paradigm beyond lupus, suggesting broad applicability to B-cell-mediated autoimmune diseases.

title: DLL3-Targeted CAR-T in Relapsed/Refractory Small Cell Lung Cancer After Platinum and Checkpoint Failure
text_chunk: Small cell lung cancer (SCLC) has limited treatment options after platinum-based chemotherapy and immune checkpoint inhibitors, with median OS of 3-4 months for third-line patients. DLL3 is expressed on >80% of SCLC tumors with minimal normal tissue expression. This Phase I study enrolled 22 patients with ES-SCLC who had progressed on platinum + PD-L1 inhibitor. The DLL3-CAR-T used a humanized scFv with CD28 costimulation optimized for solid tumor trafficking (CXCR2 co-expression). ORR was 27.3% (6/22) with 1 complete response and 5 partial responses. Disease control rate was 63.6%. Median PFS was 4.1 months and median OS was 8.2 months — both superior to historical chemotherapy (PFS 1.8, OS 4.3 months). CAR-T expansion correlated with response (responders had 5-fold higher Cmax). CRS was manageable (grade ≥3: 9%). Notably, DLL3 expression was maintained in all progressing patients, indicating resistance was TME-mediated rather than antigen escape.

title: Bridging Therapy Before CAR-T: Impact on Manufacturing Success, Toxicity, and Outcomes in Aggressive Lymphoma
text_chunk: The 3-6 week manufacturing interval for autologous CAR-T products necessitates bridging therapy in patients with aggressive disease. This multicenter retrospective analysis of 892 patients receiving commercial CD19 CAR-T (axi-cel, liso-cel, or tisa-cel) for aggressive B-cell lymphoma evaluated bridging therapy impact. Bridging was administered in 68% of patients: radiation (22%), chemoimmunotherapy (31%), targeted agents (15%), or combination (32%). Manufacturing success rate was 98% regardless of bridging. Bridging radiation was associated with best outcomes (ORR 74%, median PFS 14.2 months), while bridging chemotherapy predicted worse outcomes (ORR 58%, median PFS 6.8 months), likely reflecting confounding by disease aggressiveness. Patients receiving no bridging had intermediate outcomes (ORR 67%, median PFS 10.1 months). High tumor burden at lymphodepletion (LDH >ULN + bulky disease) predicted both higher CRS (grade ≥3: 12% vs 4%) and better response. Optimal bridging strategy should aim for disease control without excessive lymphocyte depletion that could impair apheresis product quality.

title: Antigen Density Thresholds for CAR-T Efficacy: Implications for Patient Selection and Resistance
text_chunk: CAR-T efficacy is fundamentally dependent on target antigen surface density, yet clinical decision-making rarely incorporates quantitative antigen measurement. This translational study performed quantitative flow cytometry on pre-treatment tumor samples from 312 patients receiving BCMA-directed CAR-T (ide-cel or cilta-cel). BCMA surface density was quantified as molecules of equivalent soluble fluorochrome (MESF) per cell. Below 1,000 MESF/cell, response rate was 35% and median PFS 2.1 months. Between 1,000-5,000 MESF/cell, response rate was 72% and median PFS 8.4 months. Above 5,000 MESF/cell, response rate was 89% and median PFS 16.2 months. In multivariate analysis, BCMA density was the strongest predictor of response (OR per log-unit: 3.2, p<0.001), surpassing tumor burden, prior lines, and high-risk cytogenetics. Pre-treatment with gamma-secretase inhibitor (GSI) increased surface BCMA by 2.5-fold and shifted 34% of patients from low to intermediate/high density categories. These findings have immediate clinical implications: quantitative antigen measurement should be incorporated into CAR-T eligibility assessment, and GSI priming may rescue patients with low antigen density.

title: CAR-T Manufacturing Failure: Incidence, Predictors, and Rescue Strategies Across 4,000 Commercial Aphereses
text_chunk: Manufacturing failure — the inability to produce a viable CAR-T product from patient apheresis material — affects 2-10% of patients but is poorly characterized in real-world practice. This analysis of 4,127 commercial CAR-T aphereses across a national network (2020-2025) found an overall manufacturing failure rate of 4.8% (198/4,127). Failure was more common for ide-cel (7.2%) than axi-cel (3.1%), liso-cel (3.5%), or cilta-cel (4.1%). Independent predictors included: absolute lymphocyte count <300/uL at apheresis (OR 4.2), >5 prior lines of therapy (OR 2.8), prior anti-CD38 antibody within 6 months (OR 2.1), active steroids >10mg prednisone equivalent (OR 1.9), and age >75 (OR 1.6). T-cell exhaustion markers (PD-1+ TIM-3+) on apheresis CD3+ cells were the strongest biomarker (OR 5.7 for failure when >40% co-expressing). Rescue strategies included: washout period from lymphotoxic agents (reduced failure 52%), timing apheresis to ALC nadir recovery (reduced failure 38%), and fresh (non-frozen) apheresis processing (reduced failure 25%). Bridging to allogeneic/off-the-shelf CAR-T was attempted in 62% of failures with 44% ultimately receiving cellular therapy.

title: Long-Term Outcomes of CAR-T for Primary CNS Lymphoma: Intrathecal and Systemic Delivery Comparison
text_chunk: Primary CNS lymphoma (PCNSL) has a 5-year OS of 30% with conventional therapy and limited salvage options. This multicenter study compared intrathecal (IT, n=18) versus systemic intravenous (IV, n=24) delivery of anti-CD19 CAR-T in relapsed/refractory PCNSL. IT delivery achieved higher CSF CAR-T concentrations (median 500 vs 12 cells/uL) and superior ORR (78% vs 54%, p=0.04). Complete response rate was 56% vs 33%. At 24-month follow-up, PFS was significantly better with IT delivery (median 18.2 vs 8.4 months, HR 0.45). CNS-specific adverse events were more common with IT delivery (aseptic meningitis: 67% vs 8%, seizure: 22% vs 4%) but were generally manageable. Systemic CRS was less common with IT delivery (33% vs 75%), consistent with compartmentalized activity. Among IT-delivered patients achieving CR, 24-month duration of response was 72%. These data support intrathecal delivery as the preferred route for CNS-confined lymphoma.

title: CD7-Knockout Anti-CD7 CAR-T for Relapsed/Refractory T-Cell Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia
text_chunk: T-cell malignancies present a unique challenge for CAR-T therapy due to fratricide — the killing of CAR-T cells by sibling CAR-T cells expressing the target antigen. This Phase I study evaluated CRISPR-engineered CD7 CAR-T cells with CD7 gene disruption to prevent fratricide (NCT04823091). Twenty-eight patients with relapsed/refractory T-ALL (median age 14 years, median 3 prior lines) received CD7-knockout anti-CD7 CAR-T. CD7 disruption efficiency was 92-98%. CR/CRi rate was 89% (25/28) with MRD negativity in 84% of responders. At 12-month follow-up, event-free survival was 56%, with bridge to allogeneic transplant in 64% of responders. Expected complications included complete T-cell depletion (universal, requiring antimicrobial prophylaxis and monitoring) and NK-cell depletion (CD7+ NK cells eliminated). Immune reconstitution was achieved post-transplant. CRS occurred in 75% (grade ≥3: 7%); ICANS in 11%. These results demonstrate that gene-edited fratricide-resistant CAR-T can achieve deep responses in T-ALL, a disease with dismal outcomes after relapse (5-year OS <10% with chemotherapy).

title: Point-of-Care CAR-T Manufacturing Using CliniMACS Prodigy: Same-Week Vein-to-Vein in Aggressive Lymphoma
text_chunk: Centralized manufacturing requires 3-6 weeks vein-to-vein time, during which 30-40% of patients with aggressive lymphoma deteriorate or die. This multicenter feasibility study evaluated point-of-care (POC) anti-CD19 CAR-T manufacturing using the CliniMACS Prodigy automated platform at 6 academic centers. Thirty-two patients with aggressive B-cell lymphoma underwent same-site apheresis, on-site manufacturing, and infusion. Median vein-to-vein time was 8 days (range 6-14). Manufacturing success rate was 97% (31/32). CAR-T product characteristics were comparable to centralized manufacturing: median transduction efficiency 45%, CAR expression 62%, viability 94%, VCN 2.1 copies/cell. Clinical outcomes at 6 months: ORR 69%, CR 47%, median PFS 9.2 months. CRS grade ≥3 occurred in 6%; ICANS grade ≥3 in 9%. Critically, only 3% of patients (1/32) were unable to receive product due to clinical deterioration — compared to 15-20% attrition with centralized manufacturing. POC manufacturing eliminates cryoshipment logistics, reduces costs by $30,000-50,000 per patient, and enables CAR-T access at centers without proximity to centralized manufacturing facilities.

title: Resistance Mechanisms to BCMA-Targeted CAR-T: Comprehensive Genomic and Proteomic Analysis of Relapsed Patients
text_chunk: Despite high initial response rates, most patients relapse after BCMA-targeted CAR-T. This comprehensive analysis of 89 patients who relapsed after cilta-cel or ide-cel integrated whole-genome sequencing, single-cell RNA sequencing, and surface proteomics of matched pre-treatment and relapse samples. BCMA antigen loss (complete loss or density <500 MESF/cell) accounted for 23% of relapses. Biallelic TNFRSF17 (BCMA gene) deletion was identified in 8 patients, with BCMA truncation mutations in 5 patients. Soluble BCMA (sBCMA) shedding by gamma-secretase was upregulated in 34% of relapse samples — acting as a decoy that saturates CAR binding without triggering T-cell activation. CAR-T exhaustion was the predominant mechanism in antigen-positive relapses (67%): transcriptomic signatures showed upregulation of TOX, NR4A1, and BATF with loss of TCF7 and IL7R expression. Bone marrow microenvironment analysis revealed increased MDSC frequency and TGF-beta/IL-10 levels in relapse samples. These findings define a mechanistic framework for designing next-generation strategies: bispecific CARs for antigen loss, gamma-secretase inhibitors for sBCMA shedding, and armored/checkpoint-resistant CARs for exhaustion-mediated relapse.

title: CAR-T for Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia: Lisocabtagene Maraleucel Phase II Results in Del(17p)/TP53-Mutant CLL
text_chunk: CLL with del(17p) or TP53 mutation has poor outcomes with all available therapies including BTK inhibitors and venetoclax. This Phase II trial evaluated lisocabtagene maraleucel (liso-cel, Breyanzi) in 52 patients with relapsed/refractory CLL after failure of both BTK inhibitor and venetoclax, enriched for del(17p)/TP53 mutation (78% of cohort). ORR was 67% with CR rate of 42% by iwCLL 2018 criteria. Undetectable MRD (uMRD, <10^-4 by flow) was achieved in 56% of responders. At 18-month follow-up, PFS was 14.8 months for all patients and 22.3 months for those achieving uMRD. The defined composition (CD4+/CD8+ 1:1 ratio) of liso-cel appeared advantageous in CLL, where baseline T-cell dysfunction is well-documented. Tumor flare reaction (lymph node enlargement with lymphocyte redistribution) occurred in 31% in the first week — a CLL-specific phenomenon. CRS was manageable (grade ≥3: 6%). Prolonged cytopenias (grade ≥3 beyond 3 months) affected 28%, consistent with the heavily pretreated population. These results establish CAR-T as a viable option for the highest-risk CLL population that has exhausted targeted therapy options.

title: PSMA-Targeted CAR-T with Dominant-Negative TGF-beta Receptor for Metastatic Castration-Resistant Prostate Cancer
text_chunk: Prostate-specific membrane antigen (PSMA) is expressed on >90% of prostate cancers with increased expression in castration-resistant disease. This Phase I dose-escalation study evaluated PSMA-directed CAR-T cells co-expressing a dominant-negative TGF-beta receptor (dnTGFbRII) in 24 patients with mCRPC progressing after enzalutamide/abiraterone + docetaxel. The armored design was critical because the prostate TME is among the most immunosuppressive solid tumor environments. PSA decline ≥50% was observed in 8/24 patients (33%). RECIST partial response was achieved in 5/24 (21%) for measurable soft tissue disease. Circulating tumor cell (CTC) clearance occurred in 58%. Median radiographic PFS was 4.8 months — modest but meaningful in a population with 2-3 month median PFS on standard therapy. The dnTGFbRII co-expression resulted in 4-fold higher CAR-T infiltration into tumor biopsies compared to a prior study using PSMA-CAR-T without armoring. CRS was mild (grade 1-2 in 42%, no grade ≥3). On-target/off-tumor salivary gland toxicity (xerostomia) occurred in 25%, consistent with PSMA expression in salivary glands.

title: Single-Cell Transcriptomic Determinants of CAR-T Response: A 500-Patient Meta-Analysis of Infusion Products
text_chunk: CAR-T product composition varies enormously between patients, yet the transcriptomic features of infusion products that predict response remain incompletely defined. This meta-analysis integrated single-cell RNA sequencing data from 512 patients across 14 clinical trials (8 CD19 CAR-T, 4 BCMA, 2 solid tumor targets). Consistent predictors of durable CR across all targets and products were: (1) TCF7+ (stem-like) frequency >15% in infusion product (OR for CR: 3.8, p<0.001); (2) low TOX/NR4A1 co-expression (<20% of CAR+ cells) (OR for CR: 2.9, p<0.001); (3) IL7R+ population >25% (OR: 2.4, p=0.003); (4) CD4+:CD8+ ratio between 0.5-2.0 (OR: 2.1, p=0.01). In contrast, GZMK+GZMB+ terminal effector dominance predicted rapid response but early relapse (HR for relapse: 2.3). Unsupervised clustering identified a 47-gene 'fitness signature' that predicted 12-month PFS with AUC 0.82 (95% CI: 0.78-0.86). Manufacturing process modifications that enriched for fitness signature-high cells (IL-7/IL-15 expansion instead of IL-2, shortened culture time) improved signature scores by 40%.
